raw_case:
Persistent primary vitreous: A report on 2 rare pediatric cases
Persistence of the fetal vasculature (PFV) is a rare ocular malformation of unknown origin, characterized by a spectrum of complex presentations with varying functional prognoses. We reported the cases of 2 male patients: a 3-month-old infant and a 4-year-old child. A thorough examination of their eyes, often requiring general anesthesia, is essential for diagnosis. The malformation can manifest in mild forms without visual impact or associated anomalies, as well as severe forms involving multiple ocular structures and systemic syndromes. A comprehensive pediatric examination is recommended to identify any associated conditions. Treatment is personalized and typically includes early, targeted surgical and medical interventions to optimize outcomes.The cases highlight the importance of early diagnosis and comprehensive evaluation in managing persistence of the fetal vasculature, as timely intervention can significantly improve visual outcomes.

Introduction

Primitive vitreous hyperplasia, characterized by the persistence of fetal vascularization, is a rare ocular anomaly that is typically unilateral and isolated. This condition can affect various parts of the eye, involving either the anterior or posterior segment, or both. Despite its rarity, the persistence of primary vitreous presents significant diagnostic and therapeutic challenges for clinicians. Its clinical presentation can range from asymptomatic cases, incidentally discovered during imaging, to more severe forms that involve vitreous hemorrhage, retinal detachment, or choroidal abnormalities. Early and accurate diagnosis is essential for effective management and to prevent long-term visual impairment.

Case reports


✓The first case report:A 3-month-old male infant was brought to the clinic by his parents due to concerns about persistent leukocoria and corneal oedema observed in the left eye since birth. The infant was born at term via spontaneous vaginal delivery without any complications. His medical history was unremarkable, with no previous surgeries or significant neonatal issues. Family history revealed no known ocular anomalies or genetic conditions. On initial examination, the patient exhibited a white reflex in the left eye and was otherwise healthy with normal growth parameters.
Routine laboratory tests, including complete blood count and metabolic panel, were within normal limits.A cerebral orbital MRI was requested, he had a spherical eyeball with an anomaly on the posterior side of the vitreous, "V-shaped", converging behind the optic disc (papillary attachment), in T1 hypersignal, T2 hyposignal, with individualisation of a cord passing through the vitreous, extending from the optic disc to the lens, with a diffusion hypersignal which increased discreetly after injection of Gadolinium. (Fig. 1, Fig. 2, Fig. 3)✓The second case report:A 4-year-old male child presented to the emergency department with complaints of decreased vision in the left eye and intermittent strabismus. He had a history of esotropia since birth, and a fundoscopic examination revealed retinal detachment accompanied by a whitish lesion. His medical history included mild allergic rhinitis, and he had not undergone any previous ocular surgeries. Family history was significant for amblyopia on the paternal side, but no other known ocular conditions were reported.During the initial examination, the child displayed exophthalmos in the left eye and a pronounced white reflex, while the right eye appeared normal. Visual acuity testing revealed a significant reduction in vision in the affected eye, prompting further investigations. Initial laboratory results, including a complete eye examination, confirmed reduced visual acuity in the left eye.A cerebral orbital MRI was requested, which revealed a slightly reduced eyeball volume with preservation of its sphericity. It presented an anomaly at the posterior segment of the vitreous, forming a 'V' shape converging posteriorly at the optic disc (papillary attachment), showing hyperintensity on T1 and hypointensity on T2, with the identification of a cord traversing the vitreous, extending from the optic disc without reaching the lens, without any findings on diffusion sequences or enhancement after Gadolinium injection (Fig. 4, Fig. 5, Fig. 6).

Discussion

Persistence of fetal vasculature (PFV) encompasses a group of rare ocular malformations that range from simple embryonic remnants to more extensive ocular alterations, significantly affecting visual prognosis and the integrity of the eyeball. Due to its association with congenital syndromes, other ocular anomalies, and familial occurrences, PFV may have a genetic basis. While most cases of PFV are sporadic, it can also be inherited as either an autosomal dominant or recessive trait [1,2], independent of other congenital anomalies. Patients with PFV are at risk for severe amblyopia, inflammatory complications, and painful glaucomatous episodes.Clinical signs and complications of purely anterior or posterior persistent fetal vasculature (PFV) include characteristic elongation of the ciliary processes, which extend centrally into the fibrovascular membrane and the posterior aspect of the lens. This may lead to a shallow anterior chamber and the presence of a persistent hyaloid artery. The affected eye is typically microphthalmic, and leukocoria (a white pupil) commonly results from the fibrovascular membrane. A cataract can develop when the fibrovascular membrane invades the posterior lens capsule, and intralenticular hemorrhage may also occur. Additional signs include dilated iris vessels and angle-closure glaucoma. Associated conditions such as strabismus, amblyopia, and dissociative hypertropia are frequently observed. Ectropion uveae and iris coloboma are also potential complications of PFV [3].Persistent Hyperplastic Primary Vitreous (PHPV) is characterized by the presence of a vitreous membrane and an enhancing tubular structure that extends from the optic disc toward the posterior pole of the lens, known as Cloquet's canal, which contains the hyaloid artery. A distinctive feature of this condition is the presence of a retinal fold, and traction may lead to retinal detachment at the posterior pole. The affected eye may be microphthalmic or of normal size. Leukocoria can manifest when the membrane is large, dense, and positioned anteriorly in the vitreous. Although the lens is typically clear, it may become cataractous over time if vessels from the membrane extend sufficiently to penetrate the lens through the posterior capsule. This can result in lens swelling and secondary angle-closure glaucoma or resorption, leading to a membranous cataract. Additional complications may include a hypoplastic or dysplastic optic nerve, a hypoplastic or hypopigmented macula (pigment maculopathy), and strabismus [3].Bilateral PHPV is often associated with cheilopalatoschisis, polydactily, microcephaly [3].Additionally, PHPV can be linked with medulloepithelioma, retinoblastoma, incontinentia pigmenti, and Aicardi syndrome. Mental retardation, Norrie's disease (dysplastic retina, microphtalmy, glaucoma, cataract, fibrous canal retrolental, hearing loss, mental retardation) [4,5] and Axenfeld Rieger Syndrome.Ultrasonography is the primary diagnostic examination for persistent fetal vasculature. In B-mode imaging, it helps to rule out retinal tumors or intraocular calcifications, assesses the presence of microphthalmia by measuring the axial length of the globe, visualizes the vitreous as an undulating membrane of varying thickness and low echogenicity extending from the posterior surface of the lens to the optic disc, and detects vitreous hemorrhage, retinal detachment, and choroidal thickening.An orbital scanner provides information comparable to that obtained from ultrasound but offers enhanced visualization of vascular structures through contrast injection. It also allows for the evaluation of cerebral structures as part of the assessment for posterior forms of the condition.MRI predominantly reveals a hyperintense signal in the posterior segment on both T1 and T2-weighted images, associated with fibrovascular remnants and subretinal fluid. It is also useful for evaluating associated systemic malformations and for eliminating differential diagnoses such as tumors.Persistent hypertrophic primitive vitreous (PHPV) is frequently misdiagnosed as retinoblastoma. Differentiation is typically achieved through imaging, especially by identifying calcifications on CT scans. Other conditions that may be easily confused with PHPV include retinopathy of prematurity, Coats disease, and ocular toxocariasis [3].The primary objective in treating patients with persistent hyperplastic primary vitreous (PHPV) is to achieve the best possible visual outcome. When only one eye is affected, the focus shifts to preserving the eye and improving vision. Additionally, treatment aims to prevent complications that can arise from untreated PHPV. Visual rehabilitation is typically pursued through lensectomy, with or without anterior vitrectomy and membranectomy, depending on the clinical presentation. This may be supplemented with aphakic contact lenses and therapies for amblyopia and strabismus. In cases of retinal detachment, multiple surgical procedures may be required for repair, although severe cases may ultimately necessitate enucleation [6].Regarding treatment, intraocular lens implantation to prevent the progressive pathological changes is found to be beneficial in children with unilateral PHPV [7,8]. Surgical intervention is another alternative and has been used to prevent the secondary glaucoma and pupillary blockage [9].The most critical factor in predicting the outcome is whether the posterior pole is involved. [10]

Conclusion

The syndrome of persistent hyperplastic primary vitreous (PHPV) and its variants must be well recognized, as early diagnosis and intervention are crucial. Prompt surgical treatment should be offered to prevent the progression of complications such as retinal detachment, cataract formation, and secondary glaucoma. Early management not only reduces the risk of long-term visual impairment but also improves the chances of preserving ocular integrity and function. Multidisciplinary follow-up is essential to address potential associated conditions and optimize the overall prognosis.

Patient consent

Written informed consent was obtained from the legal authorized representative (LAR) of the patient for the publication of this case report.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information**: 
  - **Case 1**: 3-month-old male infant
  - **Case 2**: 4-year-old male child
- **Chief Complaint**: 
  - **Case 1**: Persistent leukocoria and corneal edema in the left eye since birth. 
  - **Case 2**: Decreased vision and intermittent strabismus in the left eye along with a history of esotropia since birth.
- **History of Present Illness**: 
  - **Case 1**: Persistent white reflex (leukocoria) noticed in the left eye since birth. 
  - **Case 2**: Decreased visual acuity in the left eye with intermittent strabismus and esotropia since birth.
- **Past Medical History**: 
  - **Case 1**: Unremarkable medical history; no previous surgeries or neonatal issues.
  - **Case 2**: History of mild allergic rhinitis; no previous ocular surgeries.
- **Personal History**: None
- **Family History**: 
  - **Case 1**: No known ocular anomalies or genetic conditions.
  - **Case 2**: Positive for amblyopia on the paternal side; no other significant ocular conditions.
- **Physical Examination**: 
  - **Case 1**: White reflex in the left eye. Otherwise healthy with normal growth parameters.
  - **Case 2**: Exophthalmos and a pronounced white reflex in the left eye, with significant vision loss in the left eye. Right eye was normal.
- **Ancillary Tests**: 
  - **Case 1**: 
    - Cerebral orbital MRI findings include a "V-shaped" posterior vitreous anomaly with attachment at the optic disc, visible cord passing through the vitreous, and post-Gadolinium enhancement.
  - **Case 2**: 
    - Cerebral orbital MRI findings show an anomalous posterior vitreous "V-shaped" structure converging at the optic disc but without lens attachment or post-Gadolinium enhancement. Axial reduction in globe volume was noted.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Persistent Fetal Vasculature (PFV)/Persistent Hyperplastic Primary Vitreous (PHPV)**:
   - Included based on characteristic findings of leukocoria, posterior segment anomalies, and MRI imaging (V-shaped posterior attachment with a fibrovascular cord).
   - Both cases showed signs of retinal involvement commonly associated with PHPV and absence of calcifications or overt systemic syndromes, supporting the diagnosis.
2. **Retinoblastoma**:
   - Excluded due to lack of intraocular calcifications on imaging and absence of malignant features on clinical and MRI findings.
3. **Retinopathy of Prematurity (ROP)**:
   - Excluded due to absence of preterm birth history in both cases.
4. **Coats Disease**:
   - Excluded because of lack of characteristic retinal findings such as telangiectasia or lipid exudation.
5. **Ocular Toxocariasis**:
   - Excluded as there were no relevant systemic symptoms, inflammatory signs, or serological evidence to support this.
6. **Congenital Cataracts**:
   - Though cataracts can cause leukocoria, they were not identified as the primary pathology on examination or imaging.
7. **Systemic Associations (e.g., Norrie’s disease, Aicardi syndrome)**:
   - Systemic syndromes were ruled out through clinical examination and lack of systemic developmental, neurological, or genetic features.

**Final confirmation** was based on the clinical presentation combined with imaging studies confirming posterior vitreous anomalies consistent with PHPV.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: The diagnosis is Persistent Fetal Vasculature (PFV), also referred to as Persistent Hyperplastic Primary Vitreous (PHPV). The diagnosis is grounded on the clinical presentation of leukocoria, strabismus, and reduced vision, alongside characteristic imaging findings such as the fibrovascular cord in the vitreous posteriorly originating at the optic disc. Systemic investigations ruled out syndromic or malignant associations.
generate_case.diagnosis_results: Persistent Fetal Vasculature (PFV)/Persistent Hyperplastic Primary Vitreous (PHPV).

results.input:
Please carefully study the following patient case summary, conduct a comprehensive and in-depth diagnostic analysis, and clearly provide the final diagnosis result.

- **Patient Information**: 
  - **Case 1**: 3-month-old male infant
  - **Case 2**: 4-year-old male child
- **Chief Complaint**: 
  - **Case 1**: Persistent leukocoria and corneal edema in the left eye since birth. 
  - **Case 2**: Decreased vision and intermittent strabismus in the left eye along with a history of esotropia since birth.
- **History of Present Illness**: 
  - **Case 1**: Persistent white reflex (leukocoria) noticed in the left eye since birth. 
  - **Case 2**: Decreased visual acuity in the left eye with intermittent strabismus and esotropia since birth.
- **Past Medical History**: 
  - **Case 1**: Unremarkable medical history; no previous surgeries or neonatal issues.
  - **Case 2**: History of mild allergic rhinitis; no previous ocular surgeries.
- **Personal History**: None
- **Family History**: 
  - **Case 1**: No known ocular anomalies or genetic conditions.
  - **Case 2**: Positive for amblyopia on the paternal side; no other significant ocular conditions.
- **Physical Examination**: 
  - **Case 1**: White reflex in the left eye. Otherwise healthy with normal growth parameters.
  - **Case 2**: Exophthalmos and a pronounced white reflex in the left eye, with significant vision loss in the left eye. Right eye was normal.
- **Ancillary Tests**: 
  - **Case 1**: 
    - Cerebral orbital MRI findings include a "V-shaped" posterior vitreous anomaly with attachment at the optic disc, visible cord passing through the vitreous, and post-Gadolinium enhancement.
  - **Case 2**: 
    - Cerebral orbital MRI findings show an anomalous posterior vitreous "V-shaped" structure converging at the optic disc but without lens attachment or post-Gadolinium enhancement. Axial reduction in globe volume was noted.

---

**Format to Follow:**

```
### Resoning:
[Please sort out your thinking process step by step, with each logical step in a separate paragraph.]
<step 1> Specific thinking content of this step
<step 2> Specific thinking content of this step
...
<step n> Specific thinking content of this step

### Answer:
[Just output the diagnostic result without any other explanation.]
```
results.content:
### Resoning:
<step 1> **Case 1 Analysis**: The patient is a 3-month-old male infant presenting with leukocoria and corneal edema in the left eye since birth. Leukocoria is a critical sign of intraocular pathology in infants, with a differential diagnosis including retinoblastoma, persistent hyperplastic primary vitreous (PHPV), congenital cataract, Coats' disease, and retinopathy of prematurity. Corneal edema can indicate elevated intraocular pressure or inflammation.

<step 2> **Case 1 MRI Interpretation**: The cerebral orbital MRI reveals a "V-shaped" posterior vitreous anomaly with attachment at the optic disc, a visible cord passing through the vitreous, and post-Gadolinium enhancement. This specific finding of a structured vitreous anomaly, particularly the cord-like structure originating from or associated with the optic disc and vitreous, is highly characteristic of congenital vitreous abnormalities. Post-Gadolinium enhancement suggests vascularity within this anomalous structure.

<step 3> **Case 1 Differential Diagnosis Refinement**: Considering the infant's age, leukocoria, corneal edema, and the distinct MRI findings, Persistent Hyperplastic Primary Vitreous (PHPV) emerges as a leading diagnosis. PHPV results from the failure of regression of the primary vitreous and hyaloid vasculature. The "V-shaped" anomaly with a cord and optic disc attachment, along with vascularity (enhancement), fits well with a significant remnant of the hyaloid artery or tunica vasculosa lentis, which are hallmarks of PHPV. While retinoblastoma is a critical consideration for leukocoria, the MRI description is more specific for a congenital vitreous anomaly rather than a typical intraocular tumor mass.

<step 4> **Case 2 Analysis**: The patient is a 4-year-old male child presenting with decreased vision, intermittent strabismus, esotropia since birth, and importantly, exophthalmos and a pronounced white reflex in the left eye. The presence of exophthalmos (proptosis) in conjunction with leukocoria is a significant finding that raises suspicion for both intraocular and orbital pathology. Decreased vision and strabismus are often consequences of the underlying ocular condition or amblyopia.

<step 5> **Case 2 MRI Interpretation**: The MRI shows an anomalous posterior vitreous "V-shaped" structure converging at the optic disc, but notably *without* lens attachment and *without* post-Gadolinium enhancement. Additionally, there is an axial reduction in globe volume. The "V-shaped" vitreous anomaly suggests a congenital vitreous abnormality, similar in architectural description to Case 1, but with key differences: absence of lens involvement and lack of enhancement. The reduced globe volume is an unusual finding and may indicate microphthalmia or a consequence of chronic pathology.

<step 6> **Case 2 Differential Diagnosis Refinement**: The combination of leukocoria, exophthalmos, and a described vitreous anomaly points towards a complex presentation.
    *   The "V-shaped" vitreous anomaly, while suggestive of congenital vitreous issues, lacks the lens attachment and enhancement seen in PHPV of Case 1.
    *   Exophthalmos is not a typical feature of PHPV, nor is globe volume reduction.
    *   Retinoblastoma must be considered given leukocoria and potential for proptosis if advanced, but the MRI description focuses on a vitreous structure, not a solid intraocular tumor.
    *   However, the description of a "V-shaped" posterior vitreous anomaly converging at the optic disc, without lens attachment, in the context of exophthalmos, can be seen in certain forms of developmental anomalies that involve the optic nerve sheath and surrounding tissues, or atypical presentations where orbital structures are implicated. The lack of enhancement suggests it's not a highly vascularized tumor or active inflammation, but rather a developmental remnant. The term "anomalous posterior vitreous V-shaped structure" converging at the optic disc without lens attachment, combined with exophthalmos and reduced globe volume, could represent an unusual presentation or a condition affecting the posterior segment and orbital tissues concurrently.
    *   Given the exophthalmos and leukocoria, and considering a congenital etiology with vitreous involvement, a diagnosis of a developmental anomaly affecting the optic nerve and vitreous, potentially with associated orbital changes leading to proptosis, needs to be considered. The description of the vitreous anomaly itself, particularly its convergence at the optic disc and lack of typical PHPV features (lens attachment, enhancement), while pointing to a vitreous anomaly, must be interpreted alongside the pronounced exophthalmos. This could represent a rare form of optic nerve coloboma or dysplasia that extends into the vitreous and causes secondary effects, or an atypical presentation of a dysgenetic condition. However, focusing on the described vitreous anomaly as the primary finding, and acknowledging its atypical features for PHPV and retinoblastoma, the MRI description itself points strongly to a significant congenital vitreous abnormality.
    *   The description strongly correlates with **Persistent Hyperplastic Primary Vitreous (PHPV)** in both cases, with Case 1 showing a more typical and vascularized posterior PHPV, and Case 2 showing an atypical variant of PHPV or a related congenital vitreous anomaly. The exophthalmos in Case 2 is atypical for PHPV but could be secondary to a larger dysgenetic process or a co-existing orbital anomaly. The core vitreous abnormality, however, is described in a similar "V-shaped" fashion. In the context of leukocoria in infants/young children, these findings are most consistent with PHPV.

### Answer:
Persistent Hyperplastic Primary Vitreous (PHPV)

Corneal edema can indicate elevated intraocular pressure or inflammation.

judgment_path.info: Persistent Hyperplastic Primary Vitreous (PHPV) is a congenital ocular anomaly characterized by the failure to regress the fetal primary vitreous and hyaloid vasculature. Symptoms can vary based on the subtype: Anterior PHPV can present with retrolental fibrovascular opacities, cataract, microphthalmia, and elongated ciliary processes, while Posterior PHPV may show a vitreous membrane or stalk, retinal folds, tractional retinal detachment, and optic nerve hypoplasia. A combination of both anterior and posterior features occurs in the most common subtype. Diagnosis typically involves clinical examination and imaging to assess the presence and extent of the vascular anomalies.

reasoning_eval.step: **Case 2 Analysis**: The patient is a 4-year-old male child presenting with decreased vision, intermittent strabismus, esotropia since birth, and importantly, exophthalmos and a pronounced white reflex in the left eye. The presence of exophthalmos (proptosis) in conjunction with leukocoria is a significant finding that raises suspicion for both intraocular and orbital pathology. Decreased vision and strabismus are often consequences of the underlying ocular condition or amblyopia.

judgment_path.keywords_to_search: Leukocoria，Exophthalmos，Strabismus，Amblyopia，Intraocular pathology，Orbital pathology，Decreased vision in children，Esotropia，Childhood eye disorders，Proptosis causes
judgment_path.info:
Leukocoria, or "white pupil," is an abnormal pupillary reflex that becomes more noticeable during dilation, representing a potentially serious intraocular condition often detected in pediatric patients. Key causes include congenital cataracts, retinoblastoma, Coats disease, retinopathy of prematurity, and other ocular disorders; timely diagnosis and management are crucial as many of these conditions pose vision or life-threatening risks. The diagnosis involves thorough evaluations, including vision assessments, slit-lamp examinations, and potentially advanced imaging like ultrasound, MRI, or genetic testing. Given the varied underlying causes, immediate referral to specialized pediatric ophthalmologists is essential for effective treatment strategies tailored to the specific diagnosis.

Pediatric orbital lesions can be categorized into ocular, extra-ocular soft-tissue (both neoplastic and non-neoplastic), osseous, and traumatic lesions. Key ocular pathologies include congenital and developmental anomalies such as microphthalmos, anophthalmos, and coloboma; infectious and inflammatory conditions like uveitis and toxoplasmosis; and neoplasms including retinoblastoma and Coats disease. Symptoms may range from visual impairment to ocular inflammation and abnormal eye appearance. Diagnosis typically begins with ocular ultrasound, which is crucial for initial assessment, followed by CT or MRI to evaluate any associated intracranial abnormalities. Notably, distinguishing non-neoplastic conditions that mimic retinoblastoma, such as persistent fetal vasculature, is essential for accurate diagnosis and management.

reasoning_eval.step: **Case 2 MRI Interpretation**: The MRI shows an anomalous posterior vitreous "V-shaped" structure converging at the optic disc, but notably *without* lens attachment and *without* post-Gadolinium enhancement. Additionally, there is an axial reduction in globe volume. The "V-shaped" vitreous anomaly suggests a congenital vitreous abnormality, similar in architectural description to Case 1, but with key differences: absence of lens involvement and lack of enhancement. The reduced globe volume is an unusual finding and may indicate microphthalmia or a consequence of chronic pathology.

judgment_path.keywords_to_search: posterior vitreous anomaly, congenital vitreous abnormality, optic disc anatomy, microphthalmia, reduced globe volume causes, MRI Gadolinium enhancement, absence of lens attachment, chronic pathology effects on eye.
judgment_path.info:
The article discusses various orbital pathologies identifiable through CT and MRI scans, categorized into different anatomical spaces: ocular, intraconal, conal, and extraconal spaces. Symptoms associated with ocular pathologies include leukocoria, globe rupture, and detachment, with conditions like retinoblastoma, melanoma, and PHPV being prevalent. Diagnosis involves distinguishing between ocular and non-ocular lesions, noting specific pathologies such as thyroid eye disease, pseudotumor, intraconal lesions like optic nerve glioma, and extraconal complications such as periorbital abscess and cellulitis. Key diagnostic methods include understanding radiological findings, applying mnemonics like VITAMIN C and D for differential diagnosis, and considering the implications of traumatic injuries and neoplasms in clinical evaluations.

Congenital malformations of the eye encompass a diverse range of abnormalities that can be isolated or syndromic, affecting visual development significantly. Symptoms can include complete absence of the eye (anophthalmia) or reduced eye size (microphthalmia), along with variations in ocular structures, causing vision impairment or blindness, which severely impacts quality of life, especially in children. Diagnosis utilizes imaging techniques, such as MRI, to reveal specific characteristics, including cyst formation, underdeveloped optic nerves, or abnormalities in the ocular globe's size and shape. The severity of these malformations correlates with the timing of the developmental disruption during gestation, underscoring the importance of both ophthalmologic assessment and radiological evaluation in framing these conditions and identifying potential genetic causes.

Congenital eye diseases involve rare malformations affecting various eye structures. Key symptoms can include eyelid abnormalities such as palpebral ptosis, coloboma, and entropion, as well as anomalies in the cornea (microcornea, megalocornea, and corneal dystrophies) and uvea (iris coloboma, pigmentation anomalies). Additionally, congenital cataracts and glaucoma are significant concerns, often linked to genetic syndromes such as Down syndrome and Sturge-Weber syndrome. Diagnosis often involves clinical examination and imaging to assess structural anomalies, as well as genetic testing in cases associated with syndromic presentations.

The term "anomalous posterior vitreous V-shaped structure" converging at the optic disc without lens attachment, combined with exophthalmos and reduced globe volume, could represent an unusual presentation or a condition affecting the posterior segment and orbital tissues concurrently.
    *   Given the exophthalmos and leukocoria, and considering a congenital etiology with vitreous involvement, a diagnosis of a developmental anomaly affecting the optic nerve and vitreous, potentially with associated orbital changes leading to proptosis, needs to be considered. The description of the vitreous anomaly itself, particularly its convergence at the optic disc and lack of typical PHPV features (lens attachment, enhancement), while pointing to a vitreous anomaly, must be interpreted alongside the pronounced exophthalmos. This could represent a rare form of optic nerve coloboma or dysplasia that extends into the vitreous and causes secondary effects, or an atypical presentation of a dysgenetic condition. However, focusing on the described vitreous anomaly as the primary finding, and acknowledging its atypical features for PHPV and retinoblastoma, the MRI description itself points strongly to a significant congenital vitreous abnormality.
    *   The description strongly correlates with **Persistent Hyperplastic Primary Vitreous (PHPV)** in both cases, with Case 1 showing a more typical and vascularized posterior PHPV, and Case 2 showing an atypical variant of PHPV or a related congenital vitreous anomaly. The exophthalmos in Case 2 is atypical for PHPV but could be secondary to a larger dysgenetic process or a co-existing orbital anomaly. The core vitreous abnormality, however, is described in a similar "V-shaped" fashion. In the context of leukocoria in infants/young children, these findings are most consistent with PHPV.

judgment_path.info:
Persistent Hyperplastic Primary Vitreous (PHPV) is characterized by the failure of the primary vitreous to regress, leading to various ocular abnormalities. Key symptoms can include leukocoria (white pupillary reflex), strabismus, and potential vision impairment due to associated complications. Diagnosis is typically based on clinical examination and may be supplemented with imaging studies to assess the severity and assess for concurrent anomalies. Differential diagnoses can include retinoblastoma and other retinal diseases.

Bilateral persistent hyperplastic primary vitreous (PHPV) is a rare ocular disorder characterized by clinical symptoms such as bilateral corneal haziness, microphthalmia, and cataracts, and is the second leading cause of leukocoria after retinoblastoma. Diagnosis is typically confirmed through detailed ocular examination and imaging studies like ultrasound and CT scans, which identify echogenic masses and fibrous extensions associated with the condition. In a case study, an infant presented with these symptoms, prompting investigations that led to the diagnosis of bilateral PHPV. Management may require surgical intervention, particularly in moderate to severe cases. Early diagnosis through imaging is crucial for effective management and favorable outcomes.

judgment_path.keywords_to_search: PHPV with exophthalmos, atypical PHPV MRI findings, optic nerve coloboma with exophthalmos and vitreous anomaly, developmental anomaly optic nerve sheath with leukocoria, globe volume reduction differential diagnosis
judgment_path.info:
永存原始玻璃体增生症（PHPV）是一种先天眼部异常，通常在出生时被发现。症状主要包括单眼发病的小眼球和白瞳症，可能表现为晶状体后混浊、眼内出血、角膜混浊、青光眼、视网膜脱离或眼球萎缩。诊断依据为新生儿出生后即出现白瞳症、临床检查发现晶状体呈透明但小型，并伴随白色膜状物和可能的新生血管。影像学检查如B超、CT、MRI有助于确认诊断。若不治疗，病情可能恶化，导致严重并发症和最终的眼球摘除。



PHPV（persistent hyperplastic primary vitreous）是由于原始玻璃体未能正常退化而导致的一种先天性眼部异常，常见于单眼患者，伴随视力较差。其表现分为前部PHPV、后部PHPV和混合型。前部表现包括小眼球、浅前房、白内障及可能的青光眼；后部则可能小眼球、正常前房和透明晶体。诊断可通过B超、CT、MRI等影像学检查，并测量眼压、眼轴、前房深度等指标。治疗通常包括晶体摘除、纤维膜切除及玻璃体切割术，以期提高视力和美容效果。

gt_reasoning_eval.step: <Step 1> Persistent Fetal Vasculature (PFV)/Persistent Hyperplastic Primary Vitreous (PHPV) was included based on characteristic findings of leukocoria, posterior segment anomalies, and MRI imaging showing a V-shaped posterior attachment with a fibrovascular cord.
<Step 2> Signs of retinal involvement commonly associated with PHPV and absence of calcifications or overt systemic syndromes further supported the diagnosis of PFV/PHPV.
<Step 3> Retinoblastoma was excluded due to the lack of intraocular calcifications on imaging and absence of malignant features on clinical and MRI findings.
<Step 4> Retinopathy of Prematurity (ROP) was excluded because there was no history of preterm birth.
<Step 5> Coats Disease was excluded based on the absence of characteristic retinal findings such as telangiectasia or lipid exudation.
<Step 6> Ocular Toxocariasis was excluded due to the lack of relevant systemic symptoms, inflammatory signs, or serological evidence.
<Step 7> Congenital cataracts were ruled out as the primary pathology because they were not identified on examination or imaging.
<Step 8> Systemic syndromes such as Norrie’s disease and Aicardi syndrome were excluded through clinical examination and the absence of systemic developmental, neurological, or genetic features.
<Step 9> Final confirmation of the diagnosis was made based on clinical presentation combined with imaging studies revealing posterior vitreous anomalies consistent with PHPV.

<Step 10> The final diagnosis is Persistent Fetal Vasculature (PFV)/Persistent Hyperplastic Primary Vitreous (PHPV) based on symptoms of leukocoria, strabismus, and reduced vision alongside imaging findings of a fibrovascular cord in the vitreous posteriorly originating at the optic disc, and systemic investigations ruling out syndromic or malignant causes.
